content: Analysis of failure causes: Lack of efficacy accounts for 40-50% of Phase II/III failures—often due to poor target validation, inadequate preclinical models, wrong patient population, or insufficient drug exposure. Safety/toxicity causes 20-30% of failures—sometimes predictable from mechanism (on-target toxicity) or idiosyncratic (off-target). Strategic/commercial reasons (changing priorities, market dynamics, funding) account for 10-15%. Poor pharmacokinetics, formulation issues, or manufacturing problems cause 5-10%. Time trends show modest improvement in success rates over past decade due to better patient selection (biomarkers, precision medicine), improved trial design, and more rigorous go/no-go criteria. First-in-class drugs have lower success rates than follow-on products. External factors like regulatory stringency and competitive landscape also influence continuation decisions.
